content:
The idea of **information contamination** in statistical evaluation predates modern ML: survey statisticians warned that **pre-test peeking** and **double dipping** on the same data inflate significance long before large-scale learning pipelines existed.

As **data mining** grew in the 1990s, practitioners noticed models with stellar hold-out scores that failed in deployment—often traced to **target leakage** in marketing response models and **future data** in finance backtests.

The KDD Cup competitions and industrial CRM projects of the 2000s popularized checklists: split first, transform second, never tune on the test set.

**scikit-learn**'s `Pipeline` (2010s) encoded this discipline in API form: transformers and estimators chain so cross-validation refits preprocessing per fold.

Today, leakage remains a top cause of **"the model worked in the notebook"** failures in credit, healthcare, ads, and forecasting—driving feature-store governance, point-in-time joins, and temporal backtesting standards.

timeline.label: Double dipping in statistics
timeline.description: Analysts reuse the same sample for hypothesis generation and testing, inflating confidence—an early analogue of test-set contamination.

timeline.label: Data mining competitions
timeline.description: Public leaderboards expose leakage when competitors accidentally use ID columns or post-outcome fields as features.

timeline.label: Pipeline APIs in ML libraries
timeline.description: Frameworks like scikit-learn make fit-on-train-only preprocessing the default pattern inside CV and grid search.

timeline.label: Point-in-time feature stores
timeline.description: Production ML platforms enforce temporal correctness so offline training matches online inference data availability.
